title: Laurel v. Desierto
holding: Defines 'public officer' broadly for anti-graft purposes: one who performs public functions/duties by virtue of a delegation of sovereign authority, whether or not on the plantilla or receiving compensation — grounds charging the Mayor's Chief of Staff (Teope) as a public officer.

title: Llorente v. Sandiganbayan
holding: For R.A. 3019 Sec. 3(e), 'undue injury' must be ACTUAL damage — specified, quantified, and proven like civil-law damages; it cannot be presumed from mere delay. Sets the actual-damage floor for the injury element.

links.relevance: the P300 OR is actual damage; delay alone insufficient
links.note: the Llorente floor cited in Count IV

title: Cabrera v. Sandiganbayan
holding: States the three elements of R.A. 3019 Sec. 3(e): (1) the accused is a public officer discharging official/administrative/judicial functions; (2) he acted with manifest partiality, evident bad faith, or gross inexcusable negligence; (3) his action caused undue injury OR gave any private party unwarranted benefit/advantage/preference.

title: Sison v. People
holding: Defines the modalities of Sec. 3(e): 'manifest partiality' = clear, notorious inclination to favor one side; 'evident bad faith' = a palpably dishonest purpose/breach of duty through ill will; 'gross inexcusable negligence' = want of even slight care. Any one mode suffices.

links.relevance: manifest partiality via the CART conflict + insider favoring

title: People v. Henry T. Go
holding: A PRIVATE person who conspires with a public officer may be indicted and held liable under R.A. 3019 — and may be prosecuted even if the public officer co-conspirator has died. Grounds charging the insider co-respondents (Teope/Baliza) in conspiracy.

links.relevance: Teope/Baliza chargeable in conspiracy regardless of officer status

title: Presidential Ad Hoc Fact-Finding Committee on Behest Loans v. Desierto
holding: On prescription of R.A. 3019 offenses: the prescriptive period is reckoned, where the violation is not immediately known, from discovery of the offense — relevant to the Sec. 11 prescription analysis.

title: Albert v. Sandiganbayan
holding: Clarifies that the three modes of Sec. 3(e) (manifest partiality, evident bad faith, gross inexcusable negligence) are distinct and may be alleged in the alternative; proof of any one supports conviction.
